1.synthesis: 'Beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God, for many false prophets have gone out into the world' (v.1). The christological test: 'By this you know the Spirit of God: every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God, and every spirit that does not confess Jesus is not from God. This is the spirit of the antichrist, which you heard was coming and now is in the world already' (vv.2–3). The community's standing: 'Little children, you are from God and have overcome them, for he who is in you is greater than he who is in the world. They are from the world; therefore they speak from the world, and the world listens to them. We are from God. Whoever knows God listens to us; whoever is not from God does not listen to us. By this we know the Spirit of truth and the spirit of error' (vv.4–6).
1.historical_context: Spirit-testing had precedent in prophetic tradition (Deut 13:1–3; Jer 23:9–40) and early Christian practice (1 Thess 5:21; 1 Cor 12:10; 14:29). The Didache (ca. 90–110 CE), near-contemporary with 1 John, devotes chapters 11–13 to testing traveling prophets by their lifestyle and demands. The anti-Docetic confession 'Jesus Christ has come in the flesh' (v.2) was likely a liturgical test formula in the Johannine community — Pliny the Younger (Ep. 10.96, ca. 112 CE) notes that Christians sang hymns to Christ 'as to a god,' suggesting confessional formulas in worship settings. The secessionists' success in attracting followers ('the world listens to them', v.5) is explained theologically: their message resonates with the world because it comes from the world's value-system — spiritualized, undemanding of embodied ethics, compatible with Hellenistic religious preference for immaterial divinity.
1.literary_structure: The section provides the community with two discernment criteria: the christological test (confession of incarnation, vv.2–3) and the sociological test (whose audience listens, vv.5–6). The christological test distinguishes the Spirit of God from the spirit of antichrist; the sociological test distinguishes apostolic teaching from worldly teaching. Between the two tests, v.4 provides the community's confidence: the greater one is within them. The movement is from command (test, v.1) through criterion (incarnation-confession, v.2) through ground (greater one within, v.4) through counter-criterion (world-origin of false teaching, v.5) to the summary discernment (Spirit of truth vs. spirit of error, v.6).
1.theological_application: Gill on the testing command: 'By "every spirit" he means, either every doctrine that is pretended to come from the Spirit of God, or every teacher, who professes to be qualified by and under the influence of the Spirit of God.' The command is not skepticism about spiritual reality but discernment about spiritual source: not every claim to speak for the Spirit originates with the Spirit. The christological test is precise: not 'does this teacher speak enthusiastically about spiritual things?' but 'does this teacher confess that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh?' — the specific historical, physical, incarnate claim. The secessionists apparently passed every other test of spiritual ardor while failing this one. Gill on v.4: 'Greater is he that is in you — the Spirit of God, the indwelling Spirit, who is God omnipotent and omniscient — than he that is in the world, the devil, the god of this world, who, though a powerful spirit, is not equal to, nor able to contend with the Spirit of God.'
1.christology: 'Every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God' (v.2) — the test is maximally precise about the identity of the one confessed. Not 'Jesus' abstractly (the human without the divine title), not 'Christ' abstractly (the divine title without the historical human), but 'Jesus Christ' — the identification of the man Jesus with the divine Christ — 'has come in the flesh' — a coming that implies genuine physical, material embodiment. The Docetic position fails exactly at this point: a Christ who 'seemed' to come in flesh, or whose divine nature remained separated from the human Jesus, does not satisfy the confessional test. The one who is in the community and is greater than the one in the world is the Spirit who indwells them by virtue of their abiding in the incarnate Son — his presence is the guarantee of their victory over the spirit of antichrist.

7.synthesis: 'Beloved, let us love one another, for love is from God, and whoever loves has been born of God and knows God. Anyone who does not love does not know God, because God is love' (vv.7–8). The historical manifestation: 'In this the love of God was made manifest among us, that God sent his only Son into the world, so that we might live through him. In this is love, not that we have loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins' (vv.9–10). The ethical implication: 'Beloved, if God so loved us, we also ought to love one another. No one has ever seen God; if we love one another, God abides in us and his love is perfected in us' (vv.11–12). The confidence: 'There is no fear in love, but perfect love casts out fear. For fear has to do with punishment, and whoever fears has not been perfected in love. We love because he first loved us' (vv.18–19).
7.historical_context: The statement 'God is love' (ho theos agapē estin, v.8) is one of the NT's most theologically concentrated declarations. In Greek philosophy, God was identified with Goodness (Plato), Thought thinking itself (Aristotle), or the impassible One (Plotinus) — none of these could be 'love' in the personal, initiating, self-giving sense John intends. The 'Savior of the world' title in v.14 was used for Roman emperors in the Hellenistic East — Augustus was celebrated as soter on inscriptions from Asia Minor — and for Asclepius as healer. John's counter-claim is direct: Jesus is the Savior of the world, not Caesar, not the healing god. The 'invisible God' of v.12 echoes standard Jewish (Ex 33:20; John 1:18) and Hellenistic (Plato, Timaeus 28c) acknowledgment of divine invisibility; John's point is not that the God who cannot be seen cannot be known, but that his love is made visible in the community's love for one another.
7.literary_structure: The section moves from the ethical exhortation (love one another, v.7) through the theological ground (God is love, v.8) through the historical definition of love (the sending and propitiation, vv.9–10) to the ethical implication (we ought to love, v.11) to the community's mediation of God's invisible love (v.12) to the confidence this generates (vv.13–17) to the removal of fear (v.18) to the derivative nature of all human love (v.19: 'we love because he first loved us'). The entire section is a movement from indicative to imperative grounded in the declaration 'God is love': because this is God's nature as revealed in the incarnation, love in the community is not merely an ethical requirement but a participation in God's own nature.
7.theological_application: Gill on 'God is love': 'God, the eternal Jehovah, is love itself; this is his very nature and essence: not that love is God, as wicked men make their lusts their God; but God is love, love is one of his essential perfections; and whereas all the love that is in the creatures is derived from him, and all the love that is shown to him, or to his people, is a return to him.' Matthew Henry on v.10: 'The love of God is the pattern and the measure of our love; it is antecedent to our love; it is the cause and spring of our love; it is love to unworthy, ill-deserving persons. The sending of Christ as propitiation is the definitive act of this love — God did not love us because we loved him, but loved us while we were his enemies.' Gill on v.18: 'There is no fear in love; in the love of the brethren; where that is, there is no fear: so far as that prevails and gains ground, fear removes; not the filial fear of God … but … the fear of the day of judgment … He that loves God can have no dread of appearing before him.'
7.christology: 'In this the love of God was made manifest among us, that God sent his only Son into the world' (v.9) — the love that is God's nature became historically visible in a specific act: the sending of the Son. This is the letter's fullest account of why the incarnation matters theologically: it is the making-manifest of divine love. The propitiation of v.10 connects this love-in-sending to the cross: the Son was sent 'to be the propitiation for our sins,' making the incarnation not merely a revelation of love but an atoning accomplishment of it. The invisibility of God (v.12) means that the only place where his love can be seen is where it was first made manifest — in the Son who came in flesh — and, derivatively, in the community whose love for one another is the continuation of that manifestation in the world. The visible community love is the ongoing incarnation of the invisible love of God.

19.synthesis: 'We love because he first loved us. If anyone says, "I love God," and hates his brother, he is a liar; for he who does not love his brother whom he has seen cannot love God whom he has not seen. And this commandment we have from him: whoever loves God must also love his brother' (vv.19–21). The three verses close the chapter's sustained meditation on love by returning to the community's immediate situation: the secessionist crisis has apparently left behind contempt or hatred between those who departed and those who remained. The visible test of the invisible love remains the governing criterion: the neighbor who can be seen is the test of love for the God who cannot be seen.
19.historical_context: The logic of the visible neighbor as the test of love for the invisible God addresses a spiritual escapism common in Hellenistic religion. Mystery cults promised direct mystical encounter with the divine through initiation rites, often disdaining ordinary social relations as belonging to a lower order of reality. The Docetic secessionists, with their spiritual preference for the non-material divine, were prone to exactly this escapism: a claim to love the invisible God while dismissing visible neighbors as spiritually inferior. John's counter is structural: the love flows in one direction only — from God's prior love through the community's love to the visible brother. To interrupt the flow at the visible-neighbor level is to demonstrate that the prior divine love has not, in fact, taken up residence.
19.literary_structure: The three verses function as the practical conclusion of the chapter's theological movement. Verse 19 states the priority of God's love; v.20 exposes the contradiction of claiming to love God while hating a visible neighbor; v.21 gives the command that closes the loop. The logic is tight: because we love only because he first loved (v.19), claiming love for God while hating a brother is a claim to have received what has not been received — the love that produces love of neighbor is absent, and therefore the love of God has not genuinely been received. The commandment of v.21 unites love of God and love of brother as inseparable: the two cannot be disentangled without dissolving both.
19.theological_application: Gill on v.20: 'If a man say I love God, and hateth his brother — than which profession nothing can be more contradictory, not black and white, or hot and cold in the same degree. He is a liar: and so exposes himself to that character, which he has affixed to the antichrists; and herein appears the strict and close connection there is between the love of God and the love of the brethren.' The liar-category that the letter has used for doctrinal falsifiers (2:22; 4:20) is now applied to practical falsifiers: the claim to love God while hating a visible brother is no less a lie than the claim that Jesus is not the Christ. Gill on v.19: 'His love to us is prior to our love to him; his love is from everlasting, as well as to everlasting … and as he loved us freely without any motive or condition in us, so all our love to him arises from, and is drawn forth by his love to us.' The derivative nature of human love is not a weakness but the letter's central pastoral comfort: our love does not have to arise from within us but flows from what God has first done.
19.christology: 'We love because he first loved us' (v.19) — the 'he' who first loved is the God who sent his Son as propitiation (v.10). The priority is both chronological (he loved before we did) and ontological (our love is a response to and participation in his love, not an independent virtue we possess). The commandment of v.21 that binds love of God to love of brother is the commandment that 'we have from him' — from the Son who himself both commanded love (John 13:34) and embodied it by laying down his life (3:16). The inseparability of vertical and horizontal love in v.21 reflects the inseparability of the incarnate Son from the Father: just as denying the Son loses the Father (2:23), just as knowing the invisible God requires mediation through the visible Son, so love for the invisible God requires expression through love of the visible brother.
